author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
A Treatise on the Gift of Perseverance.
Why Does God Mingle Those Who Will Persevere with Those Who Will Not?
originalBook: A Treatise on the Gift of Perseverance.
title: Why Does God Mingle Those Who Will Persevere with Those Who Will Not?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Let the inquirer still go on, and say, “Why is it that to some who have in good faith worshipped Him He has not given to persevere to the end?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Why except because he does not speak falsely who says, “They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, doubtless they would have continued with us.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Are there, then, two natures of men?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: If there were two natures there would not be any grace, for there would be given a gratuitous deliverance to none if it were paid as a debt to nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But it seems to men that all who appear good believers ought to receive perseverance to the end.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But God has judged it to be better to mingle some who would not persevere with a certain number of His saints, so that those for whom security from temptation in this life is not desirable may not be secure.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For that which the apostle says, checks many from mischievous elation: “Wherefore let him who seems to stand take heed lest he fall.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But he who falls, falls by his own will, and he who stands, stands by God’s will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: “For God is able to make him stand;” therefore he is not able to make himself stand, but God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Nevertheless, it is good not to be high-minded, but to fear.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Moreover, it is in his own thought that every one either falls or stands.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Now, as the apostle says, and as I have mentioned in my former treatise, “We are not sufficient to think anything of ourselves, but our sufficiency is of God.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Following whom also the blessed Ambrose ventures to say, “For our heart is not in our own power, nor are our thoughts.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And this everybody who is humbly and truly pious feels to be most true.
